scoring_note: YES -- the ranking is inverted on severity, exactly as flagged in the launch brief, and it is being reported rather than silently fixed. Applying priority_score = news_score + base_priority literally, and NOT overriding it: Djibouti (92), HCA Healthcare (80), Freeport-McMoRan (80), Zimbabwe (77), and State Street (75) top the list -- reasonable. But Fidelity Investments, State Farm, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Kimberly-Clark, Deere & Company, and TransDigm ALL score 70 (mostly routine 401(k)/ERISA settlements, a farm-equipment right-to-repair consent decree, and a blocked defense-sector merger) -- and every one of them outranks Ukraine, Israel, Democratic Republic of Congo, Iran, Russia, Yemen, Sudan, South Sudan, Somalia, Afghanistan, and Haiti, which are ALL tied at 57 (rank 29-40, entirely outside the top-15 cutoff of 65). Every one of those 11 countries has confirmed, sourced, in-window (2026-07-06 to 2026-07-20) evidence of active war, strikes killing civilians, executions, famine, or mass displacement -- and every one of them was individually forced into Tier-1 search specifically because this exact bias was anticipated. The reason they still rank below routine corporate settlements is structural, not a data-collection failure: these countries were all assessed within the last 30 days (2026-07-18/07-19 cycles), giving them staleness_score=0, while the corporate entities above them have never been assessed (staleness_score=25) and carry higher importance_score (15 for Fortune-500 vs 12 for countries). Dar es Salaam, Phnom Penh, Guatemala City, Ibadan (all 63), Apple, General Motors, Lumen Technologies (all 60), and Detroit (60) also outrank all eleven of those countries. This is the formula working as documented, applied without alteration -- but a human should decide whether staleness-driven deprioritization of active mass-casualty situations is the intended behavior of this benchmark before the assessor acts purely off the ranked top_entities list.

top_entities.name: Djibouti
top_entities.news_summary: UN-backed IPC food monitoring network warns more than 256,000 people (25% of population) face crisis/emergency-level hunger, concentrated in Ali Addeh and Holl-Holl refugee camps.

top_entities.name: HCA Healthcare
top_entities.news_summary: Schall Law Firm announced (July 19) a securities-fraud investigation into HCA Healthcare after the company sharply cut full-year 2026 guidance on July 14, citing an unfavorable payer mix; shares fell nearly 7%.

top_entities.name: Freeport-McMoRan
top_entities.news_summary: A worker died (Monday July 6, reported July 7) in a haul-truck accident at Freeport-McMoRan's Morenci copper mine in Arizona — the third fatality tied to the Morenci operation since 2021; MSHA and the Arizona State Mine Inspector are investigating.

top_entities.name: Zimbabwe
top_entities.news_summary: President Mnangagwa signed Constitutional Amendment Act No. 3 (July 7), extending presidential terms from five to seven years, abolishing direct popular election of the president in favor of parliamentary selection, and postponing 2028 elections to 2030; opposition condemns it as a "constitutional coup."

top_entities.name: State Street
top_entities.news_summary: Texas AG Paxton sued State Street, BlackRock, and Vanguard (July 17), joined by a coalition of 10 other states, alleging the asset managers conspired to artificially constrict the coal market through ESG-driven anticompetitive practices.

top_entities.name: Fidelity Investments
top_entities.news_summary: A fairness hearing (July 9) was held on Fidelity Investments' $2.5M settlement of a 2024 data-breach class action affecting 77,099 individuals; claims deadline set for July 27.

top_entities.name: State Farm
top_entities.news_summary: An Oklahoma judge ordered State Farm (July 7) to release internal documents in litigation (nearly 900 similar suits statewide) alleging the insurer systematically underpaid/denied wind and hail damage claims, with plaintiffs' counsel alleging State Farm saved over $1 billion via the scheme.

top_entities.name: Thermo Fisher Scientif
top_entities.news_summary: Thermo Fisher Scientific announced Middleton facility layoffs (July 15), continuing a pattern of 99 WARN notices affecting 5,816+ workers since 2002, including a phased Asheville, NC closure.

top_entities.name: Kimberly-Clark
top_entities.news_summary: A fairness hearing (July 18) was held on Kimberly-Clark's $2.25M settlement of a 401(k) excessive-fee ERISA class action covering ~25,000 plan participants; separately, Kenvue (being absorbed by Kimberly-Clark) cut ~770 jobs ahead of the merger closing.

top_entities.name: Deere & Company
top_entities.news_summary: FTC and five state AGs secured a settlement with Deere & Company (July 8) requiring 10 years of equal diagnostic/repair-resource access for farmers and independent repair shops, resolving the January 2025 right-to-repair antitrust suit.

top_entities.name: TransDigm
top_entities.news_summary: TransDigm abandoned its $960M acquisition of Stellant Systems (July 13) after DOJ warned it would sue to block the deal on antitrust grounds affecting Navy Aegis and Air Force F-16 component supply.

top_entities.name: Bangladesh
top_entities.news_summary: Violence between security forces and banned Awami League supporters killed 5 in Gopalganj (July 16) following an NCP rally; hundreds arbitrarily detained afterward and 10 murder cases filed against 8,400+ mostly unnamed people.

top_entities.name: Pakistan
top_entities.news_summary: Operation Shaban militant-crackdown toll reaches 129 killed as of July 16, continuing the security response to the July 4-8 coordinated Balochistan insurgent attacks that killed 42 (38 security personnel, 4 civilians).

top_entities.name: Egypt
top_entities.news_summary: HRW reports (July 7) that Egyptian authorities are arbitrarily arresting, detaining, and deporting refugees and asylum seekers (mostly Sudanese and South Sudanese) whose residency permits lapsed amid government administrative backlogs.

top_entities.name: Core Civic
top_entities.news_summary: Citizens Financial Group announced (July 17) it will exit credit facilities to CoreCivic and GEO Group, the two largest private-prison/ICE-detention contractors, following a public pressure campaign; the move follows federal government plans to directly purchase several CoreCivic/GEO facilities.

sector_alerts.summary: Future of Life Institute's Summer 2026 AI Safety Index gives Anthropic a C+ (highest), OpenAI and Google DeepMind a C, and failing grades to xAI, DeepSeek, and Mistral; documents a pattern of safety pledges quietly weakened under competitive pressure across the sector.

Illinois Gov. Pritzker signed SB315 (July 6), the Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act, requiring frontier AI developers with $500M+ revenue to conduct catastrophic-risk assessments and annual independent audits; joins similar California/New York laws.

Coordinated protests against AI data-center expansion occurred at ~125 locations across 40+ states on July 18, 2026, citing electricity-cost, water-strain, and community-impact concerns; movement has contributed to blocking an estimated $98B in AI infrastructure projects and driven 300+ state regulatory bills in H1 2026.

sector_alerts.theme: University Title VI / DEI federal crackdown
sector_alerts.summary: NAACP LDF, LatinoJustice PRLDEF, and Earthjustice filed a formal challenge (July 13) against a $1.5B federal grant overhaul targeting DEI, healthcare, and education programs; DOJ/ED continue Title VI investigations and settlements across dozens of universities (Northwestern $75M agreement; Yale admissions settlement talks reported July 9-10; UC San Diego scholarship lawsuit filed July 16).

Citizens Financial Group announced (July 17) it will exit credit facilities to CoreCivic and GEO Group amid sustained public-pressure campaigns over ICE-detention conditions; this follows an HRW report (June 25, outside this scan's window but relevant background) documenting rising deaths in an expanding US immigration-detention system.

Fortune 500 companies cut public DEI disclosure by 65% in 2026; participation in the Human Rights Campaign Foundation index fell from 377 companies (2025) to 131 (2026) amid regulatory and political pressure.

AI-cited layoffs led US job-cut categories for a fourth consecutive month in H1 2026; roughly 139,000+ tech jobs eliminated with employers explicitly naming AI/automation, even as several companies (Meta, Microsoft, Oracle) simultaneously posted record AI infrastructure spending.

FY2025 False Claims Act recoveries reached $6.8B (record high), $5.7B of it health-care related; the 2026 National Health Care Fraud Takedown charged 455 defendants over $6.5B in alleged fraud; HHS-OIG decertified the New York Medicaid Fraud Control Unit in July, signaling escalating scrutiny of state-level enforcement capacity.

IOM/London School of Hygiene study (July 2026) documents labor-trafficking patterns across textiles, mining, and construction in Argentina, Peru, and Kazakhstan; migrants represent 21% of the world's estimated 40.3 million forced-labor victims.

sector_alerts.theme: Two concurrent famines (Sudan / Occupied Palestinian Territory)
sector_alerts.summary: OCHA notes two famines unfolded simultaneously for the first time in recorded history (Sudan and the Occupied Palestinian Territory) in 2025-2026; roughly 19.5M people face crisis-level food insecurity in Sudan alone, with 14 areas at risk of famine.

Week of July 13-20: wildfire smoke placed 100M+ Americans under air-quality alerts, Texas Hill Country floods killed at least two and triggered hundreds of water rescues, and 68 large wildfires burned across 15 states — described by meteorologists as "a summer of compounding disasters."

CPJ documented continuing press-freedom deterioration in July 2026: a sixth Mexican journalist murdered this year (Puebla), a Kashmiri journalist detained without charge over protest coverage, and a Puntland (Somalia) journalist arrested by intelligence services.
